What it checks.evidence: C1 — Four-eyes issuance approval. No guarantee is issued without a Trade Finance Manager's approval, recorded in the Trade Finance System.

Control activity.evidence: A Trade Finance Manager reviews the assembled package and approves issuance. Guarantees above EUR 5 million additionally require sign-off by the Head of Trade Finance.

Risk addressed.evidence: TFMs do reject items routinely (typically wording mismatches or beneficiary detail errors caught at approval); not a rubber-stamp.

Timing.evidence: A Trade Finance Manager reviews the assembled package and approves issuance.

What it checks.evidence: C2 — Sanctions screening. Every beneficiary is screened before issuance; the screening result is attached to the application.

Control activity.evidence: Compliance Analyst initiates, the tool does the matching — HYBRID execution confirmed.

Risk addressed.evidence: Effectiveness HIGH — false negatives possible (list-update lag, transliteration mismatches) but rare; secondary control is post-issuance batch re-screening in Compliance Ops, out of scope.

Timing.evidence: Every beneficiary is screened before issuance; the screening result is attached to the application.

What it checks.evidence: Issuance is blocked in the Trade Finance System unless available facility limit covers the guarantee amount.

Control activity.evidence: No manual override is permitted; an override requires a code path that the platform doesn't expose.

Risk addressed.evidence: Effectiveness: HIGH — no manual override is permitted.

Timing.evidence: Add PS-BGID-006 to the linked steps — the amount-vs-approved check at generation is the same control firing again.

Definition.evidence: The target turnaround is 3 business days from a complete application to guarantee delivery, measured for standard-wording guarantees with no screening hit.

Current reading.evidence: TFS records timestamps, monthly ops dashboard shows actual vs target. Current is ~85% within-SLA for standard wording (the 15% miss largely correlates with the facility shortfalls). Trend: flat over the last 6 months.

Why it matters.evidence: Add the client relationship angle — repeat business implications of missing the SLA is a real concern.

Description.evidence: Applications frequently stall at the credit and facility check when the client has not pre-arranged enough limit.

Impact.evidence: ~15-20% of applications hit a limit-related delay — the single biggest delay cause by volume.

Description.evidence: Bespoke wording adds unpredictable delay because Legal review has no committed turnaround.

Impact.evidence: Impact framing holds; add no formal escalation path when Legal overruns the informal 2-day target. Severity MEDIUM/P2 — lower frequency than limit shortfall.

What happens.evidence: The Trade Finance Officer receives the application and checks it is complete: beneficiary details, guarantee amount, currency, wording type (standard or bespoke), validity period and the underlying commercial contract reference.

Inputs.evidence: beneficiary details, guarantee amount, currency, wording type (standard or bespoke), validity period and the underlying commercial contract reference

Outputs.evidence: Standard-wording guarantees proceed directly. Bespoke wording is sent to the Legal team for review and sign-off before issuance.

Why it matters.evidence: The 'Why it matters' framing is correct.

What happens.evidence: The Trade Finance Officer confirms the client holds an approved guarantee facility with sufficient available limit. If the limit is insufficient, the application is parked and routed to the Credit team — this is the most common reason for delay.

Inputs.evidence: The Trade Finance Officer confirms the client holds an approved guarantee facility with sufficient available limit.

Outputs.evidence: the application is parked and routed to the Credit team

What happens.evidence: Legal informal target: 2 business days (no SLA, but that's the unwritten expectation).

Inputs.evidence: Bespoke wording is sent to the Legal team for review and sign-off before issuance.

Outputs.evidence: Bespoke wording is sent to the Legal team for review and sign-off before issuance.

What happens.evidence: Compliance Operations owns the investigation. There's a 5 business-day investigation target; if unresolved, the application is formally declined and the client is notified — there's no indefinite hold.

Inputs.evidence: The Compliance Analyst screens the beneficiary and the beneficiary's country against the sanctions list.

Outputs.evidence: If unresolved, the application is formally declined and the client is notified.

What happens.evidence: A Trade Finance Manager reviews the assembled package and approves issuance. Guarantees above EUR 5 million additionally require sign-off by the Head of Trade Finance.

Inputs.evidence: A Trade Finance Manager reviews the assembled package and approves issuance.

Outputs.evidence: Approvals are recorded in the Trade Finance System.

What happens.evidence: Generation amount-vs-approved check is system-enforced in TFS (built-in match). Client notification: TFO emails the issued instrument to the client and RM same-day.

Inputs.evidence: The Trade Finance Officer generates the guarantee instrument in the Trade Finance System and the guarantee is transmitted to the beneficiary's bank via SWIFT. The client's facility utilisation is updated.

Outputs.evidence: TFO emails the issued instrument to the client and RM same-day.

What it requires.evidence: The process is subject to anti-money-laundering and sanctions obligations (EU sanctions regulations, AML directives).

URDG 758 sets the rules for demand guarantees: the instrument must state amount, expiry, presentation requirements, and the demand-compliance terms. The bank's obligation is independent of the underlying commercial contract. Expiry must be either a fixed date or a defined event.

Why it applies.evidence: Applies by default when the guarantee text incorporates URDG 758 (the standard wording does). If the parties opt for a different framework, it doesn't apply. Applies to all URDG-incorporated demand guarantees, not all guarantees universally.

How it is met.evidence: Baked into the standard wording template (which incorporates URDG 758 by reference); Legal verifies the incorporation clause is present when reviewing bespoke wording at PS-BGID-003. The Trade Finance System's wording library is the operational source of the standard template.

Responsibility.evidence: TFO also routes wording review (PS-BGID-003) — auto-pass for standard wording, hand-off to Legal for bespoke. It's a real responsibility, not just admin.

In this process.evidence: TFO also routes wording review (PS-BGID-003) — auto-pass for standard wording, hand-off to Legal for bespoke.

Responsibility.evidence: A Trade Finance Manager reviews the assembled package and approves issuance. C1: No guarantee is issued without a Trade Finance Manager's approval, recorded in the Trade Finance System.

In this process.evidence: A Trade Finance Manager reviews the assembled package and approves issuance. Control owner: Trade Finance Manager.

Responsibility.evidence: Guarantees above EUR 5 million additionally require sign-off by the Head of Trade Finance.

In this process.evidence: HoTF is A only for the >EUR 5m sign-off (a separate accountability that sits on ROLE-BGID-003).

Responsibility.evidence: The Compliance Analyst screens the beneficiary and the beneficiary's country against the sanctions list. A screening hit suspends the application pending Compliance investigation.

In this process.evidence: They ARE separate. Compliance Analyst does the per-transaction screening; on a hit, hands off to Compliance Operations who own the investigation.

Responsibility.evidence: When a parked application reaches them, they decide between (a) requesting a temporary limit uplift via Credit Risk Committee, (b) requiring the client to post additional collateral, or (c) declining the increase if the client's credit profile no longer supports it.

In this process.evidence: Same-day for amounts within delegated authority, otherwise 1-3 business days. They do NOT change the underlying facility structure — that's a separate facility-amendment process. Once they sign off, the application re-enters at step 2 for limit re-check. RACI: Responsible (R) at step 2 for the limit-uplift decision when invoked.

Responsibility.evidence: Bespoke wording is sent to the Legal team for review and sign-off before issuance.

In this process.evidence: R is correct for bespoke applications — Legal performs the review and owns the sign-off.

Purpose.evidence: A corporate client submits a Bank Guarantee application through the Corporate Portal, or via a relationship manager who keys it into the portal on the client's behalf.

Role in this process.evidence: SUPPORTING confirmed — RMs can fall back to direct TFS keying if the portal is down (degraded but not blocked). In-house build on standard corporate-banking platform stack. dataClassification: confidential. RTO 4h / RPO 1h.

Purpose.evidence: The Trade Finance System — the system of record for guarantee instruments, approvals and facility utilisation.

Role in this process.evidence: systemType CORE confirmed. Add PS-BGID-003 — TFS hosts the wording library. Finastra Trade Innovation on-prem. RTO 2h / RPO 15min (Tier-1 core system).

Purpose.evidence: Sanctions Screening Tool — used by Compliance for beneficiary screening.

Role in this process.evidence: Fircosoft. If unavailable: manual list check possible but degraded — Compliance queues the application and waits unless a hard deadline forces manual override. confidential / RTO 4h / RPO 1h.

Purpose.evidence: Direct SWIFT membership, message type MT760 (issuance).

Role in this process.evidence: Criticality HIGH confirmed, no operational fallback — if SWIFT is down, issuance is paused. MX migration on roadmap but not yet live for guarantees.
